content: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm based on the concept of objects, which contain data in the form of fields and code in the form of procedures.
Encapsulation is the bundling of data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit or class. It restricts direct access to some of an object's components.
Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common superclass. Method overriding and method overloading are forms of polymorphism.
Inheritance is a mechanism where a new class derives properties and behavior from an existing class. It supports code reuse and establishes a hierarchical relationship.
Data structures are ways of organizing and storing data for efficient access and modification. Common data structures include arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues.
Hash maps provide O(1) average time complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup operations. They use a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets.
Sorting algorithms arrange elements of a list in a certain order. Common sorting algorithms include quicksort, mergesort, heapsort, and insertion sort with varying time complexities.
Binary search is a search algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array. It compares the target value to the middle element of the array.
Relational databases organize data into tables with rows and columns. SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to manage and query relational database management systems.
Computer networking involves the practice of connecting computers and devices to share resources. The TCP/IP protocol suite is the foundation of internet communications.
Abstraction is a core principle of OOP that involves hiding complex implementation details and showing only the necessary features of an object to the outside world.
Trees are hierarchical data structures consisting of nodes connected by edges. Binary trees, AVL trees, and B-trees are commonly used for efficient data retrieval and storage.
Graph algorithms solve problems related to graph theory, including shortest path (Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford), minimum spanning tree (Kruskal's, Prim's), and graph traversal (BFS, DFS).
Design patterns are reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design. The Singleton, Factory, Observer, and Strategy patterns are among the most widely used.
Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. Base cases prevent infinite recursion and stack overflow errors.
Concurrency involves executing multiple tasks simultaneously. Threads, processes, and asynchronous programming are common approaches to achieving concurrency in modern software.
Memory management is the process of controlling and coordinating computer memory. Garbage collection, reference counting, and manual memory management are different approaches.
Software testing ensures that software meets requirements and is free of defects. Unit testing, integration testing, and system testing are levels of the testing pyramid.
Version control systems track changes to source code over time. Git is the most widely used distributed version control system, supporting branching and merging workflows.
API design involves creating interfaces that allow different software systems to communicate. RESTful APIs use HTTP methods and follow resource-oriented architecture principles.
